speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is August 8, 1996. Tape number two. Interview of Yehoshua Trigor. How did religous life in your hometown compare with religious life in Vilna?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was a hell of a difference. Because Vilna was a old, established Jewish center with hundreds of synagogues. In one place in the shul [NON-ENGLISH], they had more than 10 synagogues. While in my hometown, there was only one. It was a new town. And it had only one synagogue, and before it, a little [NON-ENGLISH]. But the good part was that there was a very intensive life around the synagogue. As an example, every day, the [HEBREW]-- that means the-- the group that studied Talmud-- that included my late father and myself-- used to meet, especially in the summer, around 6pm, for one hour, to study Talmud, together with the rabbi. And the rabbi was [INAUDIBLE] Zeev Mamyoffe. Would you spell that, please. Zeev. Z-E-E-V. Mamyoffe. M-A-M-Y-O-double-F-E. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And there were about 60, 70 participants. Considering that the whole town had about two-- 200 Jewish families, it was a very high percentage of Jews educated in Talmud, who could participate in the [HEBREW], in the daily portion of the Talmud. And from this point of view, it was probably one of the better organized towns-- small towns in-- in-- in Lithuania. And Jewish life, of course, centered around the synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. And in Vilna, you said there were many synagogues. Which synagogue did you attend in Vilna?

speaker: interviewee
text: I attended in the shul [NON-ENGLISH] And I once I had the privilege of davening [HEBREW] with the Vilna [INAUDIBLE], Haim Ozer Grodzenski.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell that, please, his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Haim Ozer Grodzenski. H-A-I-M O-Z-E-R G-R-O-D-Z-E-N-S-K-I. Grodzenski. Before he passed away.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- And now he was referred to-- what was his title before, that you gave before his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Reb ?] Haim Ozer. The Vilna [INAUDIBLE]. It was a [INAUDIBLE]. Not the old Vilna [INAUDIBLE] that was 200 years ago, but the present [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And would you say that the Jewish population in Vilna was as religiously oriented, in your opinion, as the Jewish population in your hometown?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was-- in-- in my hometown, they were not all religiously oriented. But-- and those that were were mostly modern. You didn't have any ultra-Orthodox.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: You didn't have any Hasidim. You didn't have [HEBREW]. You didn't have [HEBREW]. You didn't have that. It didn't exist in Lithuania.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: But in Vilna, you had a great following of Orthodoxy. You had a great following of Zionism and a great following for Jewish socialist Bundists as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- did you have any direct contact with the Jewish Bundist movement in Vilna?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at all. Not at all. We-- we regarded them as [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why? Because they were not for the rebuilding of Jewish life in Palestine, which was our aim.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. Did you belong to a Zionist group--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in Vilna?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did. I did. And I was very proud of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a part of Betar? Or another group?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was part of Betar.

speaker: interviewer
text: About--

speaker: interviewee
text: And also Maccabee.

speaker: interviewer
text: About how many people were in your group in Vilna? I-- oh, in-- not in Vilna. I am speaking of Mazeikiai. Oh. What about when you came to Vilna?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you continue?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Vilna, it was different. Because in Vilna, when I came to Vilna, part of it was-- because it was after the Hitler-Stalin agreement, the falling apart of Poland. And when the Soviets succeeded in forcing the Baltic states into mutual defense agreements and stationing in every Baltic country, four or five bases of soldiers in each country.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, we knew that Lithuania was living, actually, on-- on borrowed time. And one of my reasons to going to Vilna was not only to seek higher education, of course, which I did. But also, in order to disappear in the big crowd, so as not to-- because I believed that one day, probably sooner than later, the Soviets will occupy Lithuania, and then they will go after the Zionists. Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Which they did.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Let's go--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was too young and was a small fry. But I know how many others were expelled to Siberia.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Before September 1, '39, before the-- World War II officially starts, what did you and your family know of Hitler and his policies?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we knew all that anybody else knew. Because we used to get, every day, three or four daily newspapers. We also used to-- I belonged to a group, also, and the attorney [NON-ENGLISH], and [NON-ENGLISH], and [NON-ENGLISH], we were-- and the-- our teacher, [NON-ENGLISH]-- we were interested in the study of political developments. It was a private group. And we used to get also foreign newspapers, including Berliner Tageblatt, and [NON-ENGLISH], and-- and-- and others. And we knew exactly what everybody else knew about Nazism. Nobody knew at that time the final solution aims of Hitler, which were formulated, I believe, in [NON-ENGLISH] in 1942, after Hitler noticed from the Evian conference that no country, including the United States, wants the Jews. So he decided that Jews are sort of fair game. Especially when the ship St. Louis returned-- was-- went around the world, came back to-- so we knew all about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew already in '39, and of course, it was-- I don't know when the ship was-- was going. But we knew all that was happening. We knew about camps in Germany. Because while there was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a control of the press and so on, but some news were always printed about camps in Germany. And you must realize that Lithuania and Germany were not on the best terms, because of the dispute over Memel, over Klaipeda.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: So sometimes they used to print in the Yiddish papers, but also the Lithuanian papers, about the atrocities, and about the-- so on and [INAUDIBLE] the Kristallnacht.

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: And others.

speaker: interviewer
text: --these are all events--

speaker: interviewee
text: So we knew all about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What did you know of Stalin before September 1, '39?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we knew of Stalin that he was a dictator of-- of Russia. And most of what we knew was negative. Except that in-- in '39 and 1940, when Poland fell apart, and Lithuania, Latvia and Estonia tried to keep their neutrality, and everybody knew that they will not be able to. And the rest of them-- the [INAUDIBLE] were far apart and unable to help. So then we knew that it would be either Hitler would occupy, or Russia. So we-- we said, we rather be red than dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] What was your reaction-- and people that you knew-- reactions to the publication of the non-aggression pact in August of '39?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we did not know everything because there was a-- there were secret clauses. And one of the secret clauses was the division of the Baltic countries. Which are the-- on the first-- in the first agreement, they're supposed to be divided, Latvia and Estonia to the Soviets, Lithuania to Nazi Germany. After a-- a week, Stalin changed his mind and he renegotiated the treaty. And according to the new agreement, the-- Lithuania was also to be ceded to Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. How did life change in Vilna when the Russians occupied the city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was mixed feelings. But many people, at the first gut feeling, were happy that it was the Russians and not the Germans. I speak of the Jewish population.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Lithuanians, of course, grieved. For them, it was the loss of their independence they-- they have regained from the czar after 127 years of Russian occupation of Lithuania. So for them, it was a tragedy. And they viewed the throwing of flowers to the Russian soldiers and the approval by some people as treachery, from their point of view, as ungratefulness from the Jewish community. Now, afterwards, of course, the Jews started to feel what Russia and what Russian rule really meant. So it was a total change. All the Hebrew schools were closed and made into Yiddish schools, state schools, or closed completely. All the Jewish organizations, except the Communist Party-- the small, minute Communist Party of Lithuania, which had 900 members, and 2/3 of them Jews. All other organizations, like the labor Zionists, the [INAUDIBLE] Zionists, the-- the religious Zionists, the religious [INAUDIBLE] that was not Zionist, the Betar, revisionists-- all of them were declared illegal. And their leadership was hunted. All the businesses were nationalized-- the bigger ones. The-- the fabrics, the factories, all industrial enterprises, 80% owned by Jews, were nationalized.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: All the bank accounts and the bank safes were nationalized-- again, 80%. And the banks themselves were all-- 80% of it was Jewish. So what I would say is that the Soviets managed to break the economic back of the Jewish community, render them penniless, so it was easier for Hitler and-- to do what he did, with the help of local Lithuanians, collaborators. Because they were, in many ways, robbed of their funds to resist, or to-- to bribe, or to, whatever, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. How did this affect Vilna University? The Russian occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they-- they started to-- changing courses, especially in economics. They added courses of Marxism and Leninism.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it took them a long time. Because Lithuanians are very-- even the-- the so-called Communists were, in fact, Lithuanian nationalists at heart. So everything went much slower than expected. Or not to the same extent as in Latvia and Estonia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. Did you take any courses in Marxism and Leninism while you were at the University?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think I [INAUDIBLE]. But I think by the time we had to take the courses, the war broke out or something, or I went to hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: But there were some courses. I know, especially-- I think there was, yes. I think I did take a-- a short course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Which, of course, we all made fun of. Nobody took it seriously.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it dangerous to make fun of this course?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you didn't make it in front of the-- of the professor, but among ourselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now what, happened to your job in the passport office once the Russians came in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it took them awhile to get around to it. After two, three months, they said, well-- or maybe even earlier-- they said, well, Lithuania is now part of the happy family of Soviet Republics. That means you don't need a-- a passport department. Because passports are given by the minister of internal affairs. They [INAUDIBLE] that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you do to earn a living after the passport office was closed?

speaker: interviewee
text: They transferred us. The small fry were transferred to the Communal Properties Department. And I worked in the Communal Properties Department. And that's where I had this situation with Dainelis and Bukauskas.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Would you explain that situation? First to identify the two people whose names you've just said and let's spell their names.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. As I was sitting-- I will come to it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: As I was sitting one day in my office in the Communal Properties, a total stranger came into my room and said, you don't know me, but I knew your parents back home in Mazeikiai. I was with the Lithuanian State Security. And now, I and my friend, Bukauskas, are on the run. And we are afraid to-- to hang around without jobs because we are liable to be arrested and sent to Siberia. What can you do for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was, of course, young, was 19. And my instincts were good. On the other hand, I was afraid lest these people might entrap me. Maybe they are turncoats and just they are trying to see whether I would be able and willing to help the enemies of the Soviet Union.

speaker: interviewee
text: The whole thing took about three minutes. And I decided, yes, I'm going to help them. And I sent them to the other room where they received jobs as home-- house managers. Because there were many nationalized homes, the big homes in Vilna, were nationalized. And they need-- they needed managers.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I send them there and they got a jobs. And one of them, Dainelis, told me that he was very grateful and he knew that I would not disappointed him. He gave me his phone number in case I ever want to get in touch with him. Of course, this was the last thing on my mind. But it so happened that a few months later-- Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --I certainly needed his telephone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell his name and the other gentleman you helped? Dainelis. D-A-I-N-E-L-I-S. His first name is A-- Antanas. A-N-T-A-N-A-S. Antanas Dainelis. And I believe, but I'm not so sure, [NON-ENGLISH] Bukauskas, I believe. But I'm not sure of the first name, so I'll just give you the second name. Fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: Bukauskas, whom I did not know at all. B-U-K-A-U-S-K-A-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now, once you helped them with these papers, did you have any contact with them before-- up until the time you call them from the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which we'll get to in a moment.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But I knew that they were working. They were working because the office of the Housing Department was very close to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's actually two doors away.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. In this period of Russian occupation, did you know about the conditions for Jews in the German part of Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew a little. In the German part?

speaker: interviewer
text: In the German-occupied part of Poland. Did you know what was happening to the Jews there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew a little. I-- we-- I-- we didn't know in detail. But we knew a little. Whatever came through the neutral countries that were there. And thought that was not too much that-- came-- came-- came out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe those that were older than I and that were professionally interested, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe they knew more. We knew a little. We knew that it was bad and that Jews were persecuted, and some Jews were sent back and forth across the-- the boundary once or twice. This I knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have contact with any Jewish refugees from Poland during the Russian occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I-- I certainly had. First, in my capacity as an officer of the passport department--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: When they came in, many of them, I screened them and I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --gave them papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the-- afterwards, some of them were young people, university students, that I met at university. With some of them, I even continued my friendship in Israel afterwards.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What did you know of Hitler's expansion in Europe in the spring of 1940?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we knew plenty. Because we knew from history, Lithuania being on the-- at the crossroads between east and west, we knew all about [NON-ENGLISH], the push towards the east. Because the Germans wanted some [NON-ENGLISH]-- they needed more space. And they wanted to subject the Slav countries, mainly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: That is, Poland and Russia, use their people that they regarded as subhumans--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: --destroy the Jews and the-- and the gypsies, and-- and create more space for-- for-- for Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to pick up any radio waves, the BBC, from England during this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not we. Maybe there were some with-- with really elaborate radios, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. After France is-- surrenders in the spring-- in June of 1940, what did you think was going to happen to Great Britain? From what you knew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we-- we knew, of course, Great Britain enjoyed, in the Jewish community, and the-- a very high standard of-- how shall I say-- a-- a-- a very high [INAUDIBLE]. They-- they were-- said, oh, as British Empire, you know, a quarter of the world belongs to them. The sun never sets on the British Empire. And we thought, also, the-- the British character, you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --will somehow, hopefully, prevail. But, we were worried because we see-- we saw what Hitler did, and how easily he destroyed the French army for the second time. The same way, by circumventing the Maginot Line and going through Belgium and Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, we were very worried.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. During this period of Russian occupation, were there shortages of any items, whether it was food, clothing--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: --anything else?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because Lithuania was a rich country. Farmers were very productive. There was no shortage of any kind. On the contrary, in the first few months, there was a kind of a little boom. Because people were worried about what might happen, and because they-- we knew the jokes about, you know, the lack of matches the lack of other things in the Soviet Union. So people started to hoard, to buy more. Hoarding up.

speaker: interviewer
text: So those that had goods could sell them at better prices. And there was a while-- a while-- that it created even a little boom. But afterwards, when the-- the Russian officers started to grab everything, and there was no replacement, and there was a shortage, then, of course, prices jacked up. Did you ever have any direct contact with the Russian soldiers during the Russian occupation of Vilna?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean in 1940?

speaker: interviewer
text: From 19-- from when they come in, in the fall of '39, until June of '41.

speaker: interviewee
text: There-- there-- there was a, I believe, there was a-- a fellow called [NON-ENGLISH], Lieutenant Colonel, the [NON-ENGLISH]-- that means the military commander of Vilna-- who apparently was a son of Russian nobility. Officers, and-- who probably did not tell, or somehow decided, probably in-- in 19-- in the early '20s, maybe, to throw in his lot with the Russians. And he and his wife were very gentle and very nice and very well-educated. On the other hand, I met some that were quite anti-Semitic.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have a particular incident that affected you, personally? That you can recall?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. A-- one-- one Russian officer, when he heard my name, Trigubov-- which-- which means, in Russian-- I don't even know how we got the-- the name Trigubov-- it means the man of the three lips. Three [RUSSIAN].

speaker: interviewee
text: So when he heard it, he thought that I was a Russian. And he was very happy. And when I told him, [NON-ENGLISH]-- I am a Jew-- he said, how dare you to call yourself such a Russian aristocracy name? Or a Siberian aristocracy name. So I said, well, I-- I don't know how I got the name. But we were always called Trigubov. And I didn't know that you-- as a Communist-- are interested in nobility, that there was a name of nobility-- well, I really, I think that's beside the point.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, no, no, no. He says, it's not beside the point. He said, [NON-ENGLISH]. I am a Communist, but I am also Russian. So he was quite-- quite nationalistic.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did this, in any way, affect your status in Vilna, this conversation?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so. Unless he made use of it. I don't think he did. Because, well, for him it was just another Jew who calls himself a Trigubov. I don't think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Did you and others living in Vilna in June of '41, or even in May of '41, think that there was a chance of German invasion?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What made you think this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we saw that Hitler was going perfectly unopposed. I mean, he was very successful. And we couldn't see how he could avoid going especially [? to-- ?] to Lithuania, which was on his way to Moscow-- I mean, to [NON-ENGLISH] and Moscow. And we believed that-- that that will be, actually, the Russian aim-- the-- excuse me-- the German aim.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any indication in May and June of '41 that the government expected this?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Lithuanian government?

speaker: interviewer
text: In Vilna.

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Lithuanian government was already a Soviet government at that time. And the-- the Soviets, well, on one hand, they expect it. And that's why they said, at that time, I mean, secretly, to Communists, that we signed the agreement with Hitler because we are not ready yet for war. They-- they certainly did prepare for war. But at the same time, there was a great deal of negligence. And I was told that the day when Hitler attacked, most Soviet aircraft were unprepared for takeoff because they-- they had-- there was no gasoline. They were-- had-- they had to be inspected. And it could have very well be a German [INAUDIBLE] trick. But this, I'm not so sure. And that-- I'm-- I also not so sure whether I knew it then or I just know it now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you when the Germans invaded Lithuania, in June of 1942? '41, rather?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, I was in Vilna, studying. And all of a sudden, I received a-- a letter from my father, that he-- he has to talk to me about a very important subject. The subject was that he was contacted in our-- our hometown, Mazeikiai, by a [? Lithuanized ?] Russian, Tominov, the son of the letter carrier. And the man said, Mr. Trigubov, you have family in Russia. I know, because you receive letters. His father was the letter carrier.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Said, why don't you take your family and go to Russia? Because this is dangerous, to stay here. So my father said, why should I go from my home, again, back to Russia? And he said, because we are very well organized here, the Lithuanians. And war is imminent. And when the war starts, many people will suffer-- the innocent together with those that worked against us.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father said, why should it be? Lithuania was always a country of law and order. There was a court. There was [INAUDIBLE]. There were lawyers.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, oh, no. This is a different time. It's time of war. And those who sinned against us will definitely suffer, and maybe, many who did not do anything wrong may also suffer. So I-- I suggest that you take your family and try to get to Russia. My father said, it's so simple. It's not so simple. You can't get out.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Excellent. Now--
